---
title: Shipton's Heating, Cooling, Unnecessary Repair, Ransom, Bullying and Blackmail Company
author: Jeffrey S. Racine
date: 2024-12-21
draft: false
feed: true
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

File this post under the "fool me three times, shame on me" category.

# Are Guerrilla Business Tactics the New Norm?

Our home has two furnaces and one A/C unit. They were installed in 2005
when we bought our home, and we purchased well built energy efficient
units designed to last. We prefer to have our equipment inspected and
serviced regularly before problems arise, rather than waiting for
something to go wrong then address the underlying issue. We also prefer
to maintain equipment that is serviceable for as long as possible rather
than dumping it in a landfill prematurely.

So, we took out a maintenance service contract that promised savings by
bundling the three units, and we pay \$29/month (we provide the
replacement humidifier pads and charcoal filters). This contract covers
annual maintenance for each unit, and the units have been maintained and
inspected annually and have performed admirably over the years.

The service contract company (Shipton's Heating and Cooling) had a good
reputation we were told (at least they did a decade or two ago), and
they generally show up as scheduled, test the units, replace humidifier
pads and clean and service the units annually. We were told that good
equipment, well maintained, can last far beyond the average life
expectancy often reported by the industry.

Unfortunately, over the past two years we have come to realize that this
particular service contract company employs what might be described as
"guerrilla business tactics", and I penned this post to link to in my
online reviews of the company for those interested in the details.

To summarize, it took three strikes to push us over the edge:

1.  Unnecessary Repairs Recommended: *Replace your collector box
    immediately at the cost of \$471.88*

2.  Hijacking/Ransom: *We disabled your A/C unit and are dispatching a
    sales rep for a new unit (in the peak of summer)*

3.  Blackmail: *Sign immediately to authorize repoirs or we will arrange
    to have your gas cut off in 42 days (in the dead of winter)*

## Strike One (Unnecessary Repairs)

In October 2023 during the routine maintenance of our furnaces, the
technician who was dispatched (Peter) told us that one unit was showing
its age (there was some rust visible on the inside floor of the unit but
no evidence of any active leaking), and in the written summary of his
visit he wrote "immediate repair, replace collector box" with a price of
\$417.60+HST.

I emailed Shipton's dispatch to schedule the repair and received this
reply:

> I can confirm the price of the Collector Box is \$417.60+H.S.T. (which
> includes the installation). \[\] As discussed, upon arrival of the
> part, I'll be directly in touch with Mrs. Racine & yourself to
> coordinate a date/time for the repair.

Here is the email I sent to Shipton's *after* Pete showed up at the
house to do the repair:

> This morning the tech arrived to install the collector box we were
> advised to fix (the same tech who serviced the unit recently and wrote
> on the attached summary “immediate repair” Collector Box \$417+HST).
> When he arrived he said “I don’t know why I am here, replacing the
> collector box won’t fix the leak in the unit”… I then said another
> tech had come and inspected the unit to get the part number, and he
> said “no one talked to me, I noted the part number.”

> I asked him why he wrote “immediate repair collector box” and his
> reply was “they tell us to write something”. In other words, he is
> claiming that the collector box did not have a leak yet he wrote that
> this should be repaired immediately.

> This is unacceptable. \[\] Shipton's has dispatched two techs to deal
> with an issue clearly indicated as “Immediate repair” wasting money on
> two trips not to mention our time and frustration. The purpose of this
> email is to request that, going forward, kindly put a note on our file
> to not dispatch this technician to our residence (Peter).

> Thanks for your understanding.

So, chalk this visit up to "unnecessary repairs recommended"...

*P.S., the furnace continues to perform flawlessly and does not leak.*

## Strike Two (Ransom)

In June 2024 a technician was dispatched for the annual maintenance of
the A/C unit (Stephen).

At the end of the service call, and with zero prior discussion, Steven
said he had completed the maintenance, had disabled the A/C from the
exterior of the house, and would arrange for Scott to come to our house
that afternoon to discuss replacement options. The A/C unit had been
operating perfectly (and, it still does for the record). I was stunned,
and told him the unit works fine. He said it is low on refrigerant and
that it could freeze up and leak. I said Shipton's installed *two*
automatic shut off devices to prevent leak damage (one redundant), which
he was aware of. I instructed him to go outside and enable the A/C unit.

We did not receive a call from Scott to schedule a visit, but the next
day were told someone else (Daniel) came to the house unannounced and we
were not home, so he left. Daniel's visit was rescheduled for the
following day, and on his second visit said the unit was old, was low on
refrigerant and could not be recharged so our only option was to
replace. I told Daniel how frustrated I was with this situation, but
asked for a quote out of curiosity and weeks later got the following
email:

> "Hey Jeffrey, I spoke with Scott and we figured out more or less what
> needs to be done. The cost to replace the 3 ton air conditioner and
> the air handler unit is. \$15,395 plus HST".

On July 3 2024 I replied to Daniel's quote, writing:

> "Would you kindly send the details for the quote you kindly provided?
> We were expecting more than a simple number, in particular the
> make/model of the A/C and air handler to be installed, cost of all
> other parts needed for the installation, and a breakdown of the
> \$15,395 total into parts and labor, etc."

Crickets... zero response to date.

So, chalk this visit up to "we hijacked your A/C unit by disabling it
and are seeking ransom in the amount of the cost of replacement"...

*P.S., the A/C unit continues to perform flawlessly, and a competitor to
Shipton's gave us the option to recharge the unit immediately or wait
until the unit stops working and recharge it then.*

## Strike Three (Bullying and Blackmail)

In December 2024 we had our annual furnace maintenance.

At the end of his visit, on the invoice he presented, the technician
(Josh) wrote "immediate repair: 1) collector box (\$470.25+HST), 2)
venting repair (\$1,399+HST), and 3) it may be time to have a home
comfort adviser provide options on a more efficient system". He also
wrote "Furnace 1 has mixed venting that is against code".

Let me remind you that 1) is the exact same issue a different technician
(Pete) reported last year (“they tell us to write something”) and that
when the technician (Pete) was dispatched last year with the collector
box to do the repair told us “I don’t know why I am here, replacing the
collector box won’t fix the leak in the unit”. So, naturally, we have
some reason to be suspicious at this stage.

As an aside, Shipton's has maintained these systems for the past 20
years. We were told at one point that some venting was not up to code
but that we were grandfathered in (many homes are obviously in a similar
situation). And, when the Shipton's technician explained that some of
the vent pipe material (PVC) is no longer used, prone to cracking, and
could create a hazard, we had them replace it with a different material
(PBS) at our discretion.

But then, out of the blue, the technician (Josh) produces a "Safety
Violation Notice" giving us 42 days to complete the repairs, which
states "If not corrected, the gas supply will be discontinued on DUE
DATE and charges may apply". And have the repairs completed by whom you
might ask?

I was out of the country and the technician (Josh) was appraised of the
fact that I was called away to deal with a family emergency. He
obviously could see that we had renovations going on and that the house
was full of trades who were calling on my wife for various decisions,
yet Josh insisted my wife sign the document authorizing the repairs on
the spot and would not leave until she did (with the threat of posting
the safety violation notice on our system and notifying the gas company
hanging over her head). She said we would like time to think about this
and get a second opinion since she had her hands full at the moment as
he could plainly see, reminding him I had been called away on an
emergency. She said that it was always our understanding that we were
grandfathered in, that Shipton's has been maintaining our systems for 20
years, so can this not wait until my return? He refused her request for
either and said he would not leave until she signed.

In order to get Josh to leave our property (recall we are in the midst
of renovations), my wife had to approach our contractor (who happened to
be on site) and ask him to take time out of his day to go into the
basement with Josh to review the issue. Only after that would Josh leave
without my wife signing to authorize the repairs on the spot.
Apparently, the technician was willing to deal with a middle-aged male
contractor but not with an older retired female. This type of sexist
bullying behaviour is unacceptable. It is also a short-sighted business
model, presuming you rely on reputation, trust, and goodwill that is.

Note the violation notice clearly states "The appliance and/or piping
does not constitute an immediate hazard", so there was absolutely no
reason to deny us time to discuss this issue upon my return.

A scan of the violation notice can be found below, you can scroll
through them to view them in their entirety (PDFs are scrollable):

::: {layout-row="2"}
![](images/notice.pdf){width="400"}

![](images/form.pdf){width="400"}
:::

I then sent the following to Shipton's three days ago:

> Greetings \[\],

> After the third service visit in a row (specifically, scheduled
> maintenance last Fall, this Spring, and now this Fall) that were some
> of the most unpleasant experiences my wife or I have had with any
> company, ever, we are left with no choice but to terminate our
> longstanding relationship with your company.

> Kindly cancel our service agreement, the automatic monthly withdrawals
> from our account, and also please send confirmation of so doing at
> year earliest convenience.

> Thanks for your understanding, I look forward to receiving
> confirmation and we appreciate your timely attention to this
> unpleasant matter.

Crickets... zero response to date.

So, chalk this visit up to "intimidation, bullying, sexism and blackmail
are our current business model"...

```{=html}
<!--

*P.S., though it "does not constitute an immediate hazard", I have zero desire to live with unsafe equipment,  and am revisiting this issue in the New Year with a company that doesn't base their business model on unnecessary repairs, bullying, ransom and blackmail (today is Saturday, next week is Christmas then New Years after that).*

-->
```

# Epilogue

In retrospect, warning signs about dealing with this company had
surfaced multiple times, and it is on us for not acting sooner. For
example,

-   A few years ago a technician was dispatched by Shipton's to do the
    maintenance. I asked how Shipton's was treating them and they said
    they were going to work for another company. They added without any
    prompting that we have really good equipment, that Shipton's advises
    their technicians to encourage clients to upgrade their equipment
    prematurely (which was one reason they were leaving), and to resist
    pressure to replace equipment prematurely.

-   A former employee (not the one just mentioned) told us that
    Shipton's use high pressure sales tactics and financially rewards
    technicians who successfully generate sales during their visits.

-   After the most recent event, a company recommended by our contractor
    examined the offending equipment, and guess one thing they noticed
    while inspecting the furnaces? That the furnaces were dirty and
    could use a good cleaning (they did not know we just had the systems
    cleaned). And when did this company examine the furnaces, you might
    ask? The afternoon of the same day on which Shipton's had done their
    regular maintenance, cleaning, bullying and blackmail, i.e., just a
    few hours apart!

Enough said.
